The Texians made camp in a wooded area along the bank of Buffalo Bayou; while the location provided good cover and helped hide their full strength, it also left the Texians no room for retreat.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994200–201-260)[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004258-261) Over the protests of several of his officers, Santa Anna chose to make camp in a vulnerable location, a plain near the San Jacinto River, bordered by woods on one side, marsh and lake on another.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994202-259)[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004283-262) The two camps were approximately 500 yards (460 m) apart, separated by a grassy area with a slight rise in the middle.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994208-263) Colonel Pedro Delgado later wrote that "the camping ground of His Excellency's selection was in all respects, against military rules. Any youngster would have done better."[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004287-264)

[![The painting shows many men, some on foot and some on horseback, engaged in hand-to-hand combat. One man carries the Mexican flag; another carries the flag of the Republic of Texas. In the background are several tents; behind them is a body of water.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/The_Battle_of_San_Jacinto_%281895%29.jpg/330px-The_Battle_of_San_Jacinto_%281895%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Battle_of_San_Jacinto_(1895).jpg)

[Henry Arthur McArdle](/wiki/Henry_Arthur_McArdle "Henry Arthur McArdle")'s 1895 painting, *The Battle of San Jacinto*

Over the next several hours, two brief skirmishes occurred. Texians won the first, forcing a small group of dragoons and the Mexican artillery to withdraw.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994202-259)[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994203-265) Mexican dragoons then forced the Texian cavalry to withdraw. In the melee, Rusk, on foot to reload his rifle, was almost captured by Mexican soldiers, but was rescued by newly arrived Texian volunteer [Mirabeau B. Lamar](/wiki/Mirabeau_B._Lamar "Mirabeau B. Lamar").[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994203-265) Over Houston's objections, many infantrymen rushed onto the field. As the Texian cavalry fell back, Lamar remained behind to rescue another Texian who had been thrown from his horse; Mexican officers "reportedly applauded" his bravery.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994204-266) Houston was irate that the infantry had disobeyed his orders and given Santa Anna a better estimate of their strength; the men were equally upset that Houston had not allowed a full battle.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006267-267)

Throughout the night, Mexican troops worked to fortify their camp, creating [breastworks](/wiki/Breastworks "Breastworks") out of everything they could find, including saddles and brush.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994205-268) At 9 a.m. on April 21, Cos arrived with 540 reinforcements, bringing the Mexican force to 1,200 men, which outnumbered the Texians.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994206-269) Cos's men were raw recruits rather than experienced soldiers, and they had marched steadily for more than 24 hours, with no rest and no food.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004292-270) As the morning wore on with no Texian attack, Mexican officers lowered their guard. By afternoon, Santa Anna had given permission for Cos's men to sleep; his own tired troops also took advantage of the time to rest, eat, and bathe.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004328-271)

Not long after the Mexican reinforcements arrived, Houston ordered Smith to destroy [Vince's Bridge](/wiki/Vince%27s_Bridge "Vince's Bridge"), 5 miles (8.0 km) away, to slow down any further Mexican reinforcements.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994207-272) At 4 p.m. the Texians began creeping quietly through the tall grass, pulling the cannon behind them.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994312–318-273) The Texian cannon fired at 4:30, beginning the [battle of San Jacinto](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Jacinto "Battle of San Jacinto").[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004210-274) After a single volley, Texians broke ranks and swarmed over the Mexican breastworks to engage in hand-to-hand combat. Mexican soldiers were taken completely by surprise. Santa Anna, Castrillón, and Almonte yelled often conflicting orders, attempting to organize their men into some form of defense.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004211-275) Within 18 minutes, Mexican soldiers abandoned their campsite and fled for their lives.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006271-276) The killing lasted for hours.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994213-277)

Many Mexican soldiers retreated through the marsh to Peggy Lake.[[n]](#cite_note-279) Texian riflemen stationed themselves on the banks and shot at anything that moved. Many Texian officers, including Houston and Rusk, attempted to stop the slaughter, but they were unable to gain control of the men. Texians continued to chant "Remember the Alamo! Remember Goliad!" while frightened Mexican infantry yelled "Me no Alamo!" and begged for mercy to no avail.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994211–215-280) In what historian Davis called "one of the most one-sided victories in history",[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006274-281) 650 Mexican soldiers were killed and 300 captured.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994215-282) Eleven Texians died, with 30 others, including Houston, wounded.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004364-283)

Although Santa Anna's troops had been thoroughly vanquished, they did not represent the bulk of the Mexican army in Texas. An additional 4,000 troops remained under the commands of Urrea and General [Vicente Filisola](/wiki/Vicente_Filisola "Vicente Filisola").[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006272-284) Texians had won the battle due to mistakes made by Santa Anna, and Houston was well aware that his troops would have little hope of repeating their victory against Urrea or Filisola.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006273-285) As darkness fell, a large group of prisoners were led into camp. Houston initially mistook the group for Mexican reinforcements and shouted out that all was lost.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006276-286)

### Mexican retreat and surrender

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Revolution&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mexican retreat and surrender")]

[![A group of men are gathered under a large tree. One man lays on the ground under the trees, with his bare foot exposed.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/SantaAnnaSurrender.jpg/250px-SantaAnnaSurrender.jpg)](/wiki/File:SantaAnnaSurrender.jpg)

*"Surrender of Santa Anna"* by [William Henry Huddle](/wiki/William_Henry_Huddle "William Henry Huddle") shows the Mexican president and general surrendering to a wounded Sam Houston, [battle of San Jacinto](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Jacinto "Battle of San Jacinto")

Santa Anna had successfully escaped towards Vince's Bridge.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004353-287) Finding the bridge destroyed, he hid in the marsh and was captured the following day.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994215-282) He was brought before Houston, who had been shot in the ankle[[o]](#cite_note-289) and badly wounded.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006272-284) Texian soldiers gathered around, calling for the Mexican general's immediate execution. Bargaining for his life, Santa Anna suggested that he order the remaining Mexican troops to stay away.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994216-290) In a letter to Filisola, who was now the senior Mexican official in Texas, Santa Anna wrote that "yesterday evening [we] had an unfortunate encounter" and ordered his troops to retreat to Béxar and await further instructions.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006273-285)

Urrea urged Filisola to continue the campaign. He was confident that he could successfully challenge the Texian troops. According to Hardin, "Santa Anna had presented Mexico with one military disaster; Filisola did not wish to risk another."[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994245-291) Spring rains ruined the ammunition and rendered the roads almost impassable, with troops sinking to their knees in mud. Mexican troops were soon out of food, and began to fall ill from dysentery and other diseases.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006277-292) Their supply lines had completely broken down, leaving no hope of further reinforcements.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994246-293) Filisola later wrote that "Had the enemy met us under these cruel circumstances, on the only road that was left, no alternative remained but to die or surrender at discretion".[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006277-292)

For several weeks after San Jacinto, Santa Anna continued to negotiate with Houston, Rusk, and then Burnet.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006279-294) Santa Anna suggested two treaties, a public version of promises made between the two countries, and a private version that included Santa Anna's personal agreements. The [Treaties of Velasco](/wiki/Treaties_of_Velasco "Treaties of Velasco") required that all Mexican troops retreat south of the Rio Grande and that all private property – including slaves – be respected and restored. Prisoners-of-war would be released unharmed, and Santa Anna would be given passage to Veracruz immediately. He secretly promised to persuade the Mexican Congress to acknowledge the Republic of Texas and to recognize the Rio Grande as the border between the two countries.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006282-295)

When Urrea began marching south in mid-May, many families from San Patricio who had supported the Mexican army went with him. When Texian troops arrived in early June, they found only 20 families remaining. The area around San Patricio and Refugio suffered a "noticeable depopulation" in the Republic of Texas years.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992180-296) Although the treaty had specified that Urrea and Filisola would return any slaves their armies had sheltered, Urrea refused to comply. Many former slaves followed the army to Mexico, where they could be free.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992245-297) By late May the Mexican troops had crossed the Nueces.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006277-292) Filisola fully expected that the defeat was temporary and that a second campaign would be launched to retake Texas.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994246-293)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Revolution&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Revolution&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Military")]

When Mexican authorities received word of Santa Anna's defeat at San Jacinto, flags across the country were lowered to half staff and draped in mourning.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008103-298) Denouncing any agreements signed by Santa Anna, a prisoner of war, the Mexican authorities refused to recognize the Republic of Texas.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006288-299) Filisola was derided for leading the retreat and quickly replaced by Urrea. Within months, Urrea gathered 6,000 troops in Matamoros, poised to reconquer Texas. However, the renewed Mexican invasion of Texas never materialized as Urrea's army was redirected to address continued federalist rebellions in other state regions in Mexico.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006289-300)

Most in Texas assumed the Mexican army would return quickly.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992201-301) So many American volunteers flocked to the Texian army in the months after the victory at San Jacinto that the Texian government was unable to maintain an accurate list of enlistments.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006291-302) Out of caution, Béxar remained under martial law throughout 1836. Rusk ordered that all *Tejanos* in the area between the Guadalupe and Nueces Rivers migrate either to east Texas or to Mexico.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992201-301) Some residents who refused to comply were forcibly removed. New Anglo settlers moved in and used threats and legal maneuvering to take over the land once settled by *Tejanos*.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006288-299)[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992206-303) Over the next several years, hundreds of *Tejano* families resettled in Mexico.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006288-299)

For years, Mexican authorities used the reconquering of Texas as an excuse for implementing new taxes and making the army the budgetary priority of the impoverished nation.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVazquez1985315-304) Only sporadic skirmishes resulted.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008125-305) Larger expeditions were postponed as military funding was consistently diverted to other rebellions, out of fear that those regions would ally with Texas and further fragment the country.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVazquez1985315-304)[[p]](#cite_note-308) The northern Mexican states, the focus of the Matamoros Expedition, briefly launched an independent [Republic of the Rio Grande](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Rio_Grande "Republic of the Rio Grande") in 1839.[[293]](#cite_note-reid169-309) The same year, the Mexican Congress considered a law to declare it treasonous to speak positively of Texas.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008123-310) In June 1843, leaders of the two nations declared an armistice.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008127-311)

### Republic of Texas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Revolution&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Republic of Texas")]

[![The land comprising Mexican Texas, between the Red, Sabine, and Nueces Rivers, is shaded yellow. The land between this boundary and the Rio Grande on the south and the Arkansas River on the north is shaded green and marked as "claimed territory".](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Wpdms_republic_of_texas.svg/250px-Wpdms_republic_of_texas.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wpdms_republic_of_texas.svg)

The Republic of Texas, including the disputed territory

On June 1, 1836, Santa Anna boarded a ship to travel back to Mexico. For the next two days, crowds of Texian soldiers, many of whom had arrived that week from the United States, gathered to demand his execution. Lamar, by now promoted to Secretary of War, gave a speech insisting that "Mobs must not intimidate the government. We want no [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") in Texas!", but on June 4 soldiers seized Santa Anna and put him under military arrest.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992104-312) According to Lack, "the shock of having its foreign policy overturned by popular rebellion had weakened the interim government irrevocably".[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992105-313) A group of soldiers staged an unsuccessful coup in mid-July.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992106-314) In response, Burnet called for elections to ratify the constitution and elect a Congress,[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992107-315) the sixth set of leaders for Texas in a 12-month period.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992256-316) Voters overwhelmingly chose Houston the first [president](/wiki/President_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "President of the Republic of Texas"), ratified the constitution drawn up by the Convention of 1836, and approved a resolution to request annexation to the United States.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006295-317) Houston issued an executive order sending Santa Anna to [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and from there he was soon sent home.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006301-318)

During his absence, Santa Anna had been deposed. Upon his arrival, the Mexican press wasted no time in attacking him for his cruelty towards those prisoners executed at Goliad. In May 1837, Santa Anna requested an inquiry into the event.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVazquez1985316-319) The judge determined the inquiry was only for fact-finding and took no action; press attacks in both Mexico and the United States continued.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVazquez1985317-320) Santa Anna was disgraced until the following year, when he became a hero of the [Pastry War](/wiki/Pastry_War "Pastry War").[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008116-321)

The first Texas Legislature declined to ratify the treaty Houston had signed with the Cherokee, declaring he had no authority to make any promises.[[135]](#cite_note-Haley107and108-143) Although the Texian interim governments had vowed to eventually compensate citizens for goods that were impressed during the war efforts, for the most part livestock and horses were not returned.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992232-322) Veterans were guaranteed land bounties; in 1879, surviving Texian veterans who served more than three months from October 1, 1835, through January 1, 1837, were guaranteed an additional 1,280 acres (520 ha) in public lands.[[307]](#cite_note-miller343-323) Over 1.3 million acres (530 thousand ha) of land were granted; some of this was in [Greer County](/wiki/Greer_County,_Oklahoma "Greer County, Oklahoma"), which was later determined to be part of [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma").[[308]](#cite_note-miller344and6-324)

Republic of Texas policies changed the status of many living in the region. The constitution forbade free blacks from living in Texas permanently. Individual slaves could only be freed by congressional order, and the newly emancipated person would then be forced to leave Texas.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992250–251-325) Women also lost significant legal rights under the new constitution, which substituted English common law practices for the traditional Spanish law system. Under common law, the idea of [community property](/wiki/Community_property "Community property") was eliminated, and women no longer had the ability to act for themselves legally – to sign contracts, own property, or sue. Some of these rights were restored in 1845, when Texas added them to the new state constitution.[[310]](#cite_note-stuntz56-326) During the Republic of Texas years, *Tejanos* likewise faced much discrimination.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994250-327)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Revolution&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Mexican authorities blamed the loss of Texas on United States intervention.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008103-298) Although the United States remained officially neutral,[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994177-328) 40 percent of the men who enlisted in the Texian army from October 1 through April 21 arrived from the United States after hostilities began.[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992132-329) More than 200 of the volunteers were members of the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"); none were punished when they returned to their posts.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994177-328) American individuals also provided supplies and money to the cause of Texian independence.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992254-330)< For the next decade, Mexican politicians frequently denounced the United States for the involvement of its citizens.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008114-331)

The United States agreed to recognize the Republic of Texas in March 1837 but declined to annex the territory.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008121-332) The fledgling republic now attempted to persuade European nations to agree to recognition.[[317]](#cite_note-barker185-333) In late 1839 France recognized the Republic of Texas after being convinced it would make a fine trading partner.[[318]](#cite_note-barker187-334)

For several decades, official British policy was to maintain strong ties with Mexico in the hopes that the country could stop the United States from expanding further.[[319]](#cite_note-reid10-335) When the Texas Revolution erupted, Great Britain had declined to become involved, officially expressing confidence that Mexico could handle its own affairs.[[320]](#cite_note-reid76-336) In 1840, after years in which the Republic of Texas was neither annexed by the United States nor reabsorbed into Mexico, Britain signed a treaty to recognize the nation and act as a mediator to help Texas gain recognition from Mexico.[[321]](#cite_note-reid157-337)

The United States voted to [annex Texas](/wiki/Annexation_of_Texas "Annexation of Texas") as the 28th state in March 1845.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008139-338) Two months later, Mexico agreed to recognize the Republic of Texas as long as there was no annexation to the United States.[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008146-339) On July 4, 1845, Texians voted for annexation.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008147-340) This prompted the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), in which Mexico lost almost 55 percent of its territory to the United States and formally relinquished its claim on Texas.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson2008177-341)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Revolution&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Further information: [Legacy of the Battle of the Alamo](/wiki/Legacy_of_the_Battle_of_the_Alamo "Legacy of the Battle of the Alamo")

[![A tall stone column, which widens as it meets the base, a large rectangular building with no windows.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/San_Jacinto_Monument_-_Full.jpg/250px-San_Jacinto_Monument_-_Full.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Jacinto_Monument_-_Full.jpg)

[The San Jacinto Monument](/wiki/San_Jacinto_Monument "San Jacinto Monument") is a memorial to the men who died during the Texas Revolution.

Although no new fighting techniques were introduced during the Texas Revolution,[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006305-342) casualty figures were quite unusual for the time. Generally, in 19th-century warfare, the number of wounded outnumbered those killed by a factor of two or three. From October 1835 through April 1836, approximately 1,000 Mexican and 600 Texian soldiers died, while the wounded numbered 400 Mexican and 350 Texian. In addition, around 700 Mexican soldiers were captured.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003165-343) The deviation from the norm was due to Santa Anna's decision to label Texian rebels as traitors and to the Texian desire for revenge.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavis2006302-344)

During the revolution, Texian soldiers gained a reputation for courage and militance.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994250-327)[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992132-329) Lack points out that fewer than five percent of the Texian population enrolled in the army during the war, a fairly low rate of participation[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELack1992132-329) Texian soldiers recognized that the Mexican cavalry was far superior to their own. Over the next decade, the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Ranger_Division "Texas Ranger Division") borrowed Mexican cavalry tactics and adopted the [Spanish saddle](/wiki/Western_saddle "Western saddle") and spurs, the [lasso](/wiki/Lasso "Lasso"), and the bandana.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardin1994248-345)

The [Texas Veterans Association](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Veterans_Association&action=edit&redlink=1 "Texas Veterans Association (page does not exist)"), composed solely of revolutionary veterans living in Texas, was active from 1873 through 1901 and played a key role in convincing the legislature to create a monument to honor the San Jacinto veterans.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004423-346) In the late 19th century, the Texas Legislature purchased the San Jacinto battlesite, which is now home to the [San Jacinto Monument](/wiki/San_Jacinto_Monument "San Jacinto Monument"), the tallest stone column monument in the world.[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2004425–426-347) In the early 20th century, the Texas Legislature purchased the Alamo Mission,[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETodishTodishSpring1998199-348) now an official state shrine.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmondson2000423-349) In front of the church, in the center of Alamo Plaza, stands a [cenotaph](/wiki/Alamo_Cenotaph "Alamo Cenotaph") designed by [Pompeo Coppini](/wiki/Pompeo_Coppini "Pompeo Coppini") which commemorates the defenders who died during the battle.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmondson2000418-350) More than 2.5 million people visit the Alamo every year.[[335]](#cite_note-351)

The Texas Revolution has been the subject of poetry and of many books, plays and films. Most English-language treatments reflect the perspectives of the Anglos and are centered primarily on the battle of the Alamo.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham198535,_42-352) From the first novel depicting events of the revolution, 1838's *Mexico versus Texas*, through the mid-20th century, most works contained themes of [anticlericalism](/wiki/Anticlericalism "Anticlericalism") and [racism](/wiki/Racism "Racism"), depicting the battle as a fight for freedom between good (Anglo Texian) and evil (Mexican).[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham198536–39,_44-353) In both English- and Spanish-language literature, the Alamo is often compared to the [battle of Thermopylae](/wiki/Battle_of_Thermopylae "Battle of Thermopylae").[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham198541–42-354) The 1950s [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") miniseries *[Davy Crockett](/wiki/Davy_Crockett_(miniseries) "Davy Crockett (miniseries)")*, which took considerable license with its source material, inspired worldwide interest in the battle.[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham198553-355) Within several years, [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne") directed and starred in one of the best-known and perhaps least historically accurate film versions, *[The Alamo](/wiki/The_Alamo_(1960_film) "The Alamo (1960 film)")* (1960).[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETodishTodishSpring1998188-356)[[q]](#cite_note-357) Notably, this version made the first attempt to leave behind racial stereotypes; it was still banned in Mexico.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham198559-358) In the late 1970s, works about the Alamo began to explore *Tejano* perspectives, which had been all but extinguished even from textbooks about the revolution, and to explore the revolution's links to slavery.[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham198562–63-359)
